worth_it_explanation: Who can reach the Microsoft Monetize console, and what they can do once inside, decides how much damage a mistake or a departed employee can cause. A console seat carries the power to change floor pricing, demand partner deals, and inventory settings that move real revenue, and an API credential can do the same at scale without a person watching. The check is a review of the access already granted rather than a rebuild, so it costs reading time against the account's user list and credential inventory, and the finding points to a specific seat or token a publisher can remove or scope down.
intro: Microsoft Monetize (formerly Xandr Monetize) is the sell-side platform a publisher uses to run its ad server, set floor pricing, and manage demand partners, so a seat in its console is a seat at the controls of the revenue operation. This subcategory is about who holds that access and how widely it reaches, both the human users in the console and the application programming interface (API) credentials that act on the account without a person present. These checks confirm that access follows least privilege, that departed staff no longer hold seats, and that API credentials are scoped to what they need and rotated rather than shared and left in place.

audit_checks.title: Console access does not follow least privilege
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A console seat in Microsoft Monetize can change floor pricing, demand partner deals, and inventory settings that move revenue. When more users hold administrative access than the work requires, or departed staff keep active seats, the surface for an accidental misconfiguration or an unauthorized change widens, and a single over-privileged account becomes a single point of failure for the monetization setup.

educate.base:
Least privilege is the principle that a user holds the narrowest access that still lets them do their job, and no more. In Microsoft Monetize (formerly Xandr Monetize), the console controls the ad server, floor pricing, demand partner deals, and inventory configuration, so an administrative seat carries the power to change the settings that decide how a publisher's inventory is sold and at what price. The more people who hold that level of access, the more chances there are for an accidental change to pricing or targeting, and the larger the blast radius if any one account is compromised.

The two patterns that pull a publisher away from least privilege are over-provisioning and stale access. Over-provisioning is granting administrative or broad write access to users who only need to read reports or manage a single function, often because assigning the broadest role was faster than choosing a narrower one. Stale access is a seat that should have been removed but was not, most commonly an employee who left the company or a contractor whose engagement ended, whose login still works and still carries whatever access it had on the last day.

Neither pattern announces itself. The account keeps running, the reports keep loading, and the over-broad or orphaned seat sits unused until the day it is used by mistake or by someone who should not have it. A periodic review of the user list against the work each person actually does is how a publisher keeps the gap from widening.

A few signs point to access that has drifted from least privilege:
  • More users hold administrative or broad write access than the number of people who actually configure pricing, inventory, or demand
  • The user list includes names of people who have left the company or contractors whose engagement has ended
  • Roles were assigned by granting the broadest option rather than matching access to each person's function
  • No record exists of when the user list was last reviewed against current staff and responsibilities
You may want to review the console user list against the people who currently need access and the function each one performs. One approach is to set a recurring review where every active seat is confirmed against a current employee or contractor and the role each holds is matched to the work they do.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media operation, the people who touch the monetization console often span an ad operations team, a revenue or yield manager, and sometimes editorial or product staff who only need to read performance. You may want to separate the seats that configure pricing and demand from the seats that only read reports, so a wider group can see results without holding the power to change how inventory is sold.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content operation, monetization is usually a smaller part of the business, so the console may have only a few legitimate users alongside an agency or contractor brought in to set it up. You may want to confirm that any agency or contractor seat is still needed after the setup work is done, and remove or scope it down once the engagement ends rather than leaving it active.

educate.learn_more_note: Microsoft Monetize's detailed documentation on console user roles and seat management is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the console user list against current staff and the function each person performs, and look for administrative seats that exceed the work or belong to people no longer with the account.

monetize_ui.label: Microsoft Monetize console
steps: Open the user management area of the Microsoft Monetize console and list every active user with the role or access level each one holds.
For each user, record the function they perform and whether their access level matches that function or exceeds it.
Cross-check the list against a current roster of employees and contractors, and flag any seat that belongs to a person who has left the company or whose engagement has ended.
Record whether any review of the user list against current staff has happened and when it last took place.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each active console user, the access level they hold, and the function they perform, then checks each seat against a current staff roster. A user list where access levels match each person's function and every seat belongs to a current employee or contractor passes. A list that includes over-broad administrative access, seats belonging to departed staff, or no record of a recent review is a fail recorded with the specific seats that exceed the work or no longer belong.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each console user, their access level, and whether the seat still belongs to current staff above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that console access in {{site_name}}'s Microsoft Monetize account does not follow least privilege, with administrative or broad access held by more users than the work requires, seats belonging to people no longer with the account, or both. A monetization console seat can change floor pricing, demand partner deals, and inventory settings, so each over-broad or orphaned seat widens the surface for an accidental change or an unauthorized one. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the user list against current staff, matching each person's access to the function they perform, and removing seats that belong to departed employees or ended engagements. After the review, {{voice_recommend}} setting a recurring check so the user list is confirmed against current staff on a regular schedule rather than only when a problem surfaces.

audit_checks.title: API credentials are shared, broadly scoped, or not rotated
audit_checks.impact_rationale: An API credential can change pricing, inventory, and demand settings at scale without a person watching, so a token that is shared across people or systems, scoped wider than its job, or never rotated carries the same power as an administrative seat with less accountability. A shared or stale credential cannot be traced to one owner and cannot be revoked without breaking everything that uses it, which leaves a publisher unable to cut off access cleanly when a person leaves or a system is retired.

educate.base:
Microsoft Monetize (formerly Xandr Monetize) exposes an application programming interface (API) that lets systems read and change account settings without a person logging in, which is how publishers automate reporting, pricing updates, and inventory management. An API credential, the token or key that authenticates those calls, carries real power. A credential scoped for write access can change floor prices, demand partner deals, and inventory configuration at machine speed and machine scale, with no one watching the console while it happens.

Three habits turn an API credential from a useful tool into a standing risk. The first is sharing, where one credential is used by several people or several systems, so an action taken through it cannot be traced to a single owner and the credential cannot be revoked without breaking everything that depends on it. The second is over-scoping, where a credential built for a read-only reporting job is granted write access it never uses, so a leak or misuse of that token can change settings rather than only read them. The third is never rotating, where a credential is created once and left in place for years, so a key that may have been exposed in a log, a screenshot, or a departed employee's notes stays valid indefinitely.

Scoping and rotation are the two controls that contain this risk. Scoping means each credential holds only the access its job requires, so a reporting integration gets read access and nothing more. Rotation means credentials are replaced on a schedule and whenever a person who knew them leaves, so an exposed key has a limited useful life rather than an open-ended one. Together they keep an API credential closer to a single-purpose tool than a master key.

A few signs point to API credentials that need attention:
  • One credential is used across multiple people or multiple systems rather than one credential per integration
  • A credential built for reporting or another read-only job holds write access it does not need
  • Credentials have never been rotated, or no schedule exists for replacing them
  • No inventory exists of which credentials are active, what each one is for, and who owns it
You may want to inventory the active API credentials, confirm each one is scoped to its job, and set a rotation schedule. One approach is to issue a separate credential per integration, grant each the narrowest access its task requires, and rotate them on a fixed cadence and whenever someone who held them departs.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media operation, API access often drives a reporting pipeline that feeds a revenue dashboard or a data warehouse, a job that needs read access only. You may want to confirm the reporting credential is scoped read-only rather than carrying the write access that a pricing or inventory integration would need, so a leak of the reporting token cannot change how inventory is sold.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content operation, an agency or platform partner may hold the API credential that manages the monetization integration. You may want to confirm that credential is owned and tracked rather than shared informally, and that it is rotated when the agency relationship changes or the engagement ends, so access does not outlive the partnership.

learn_more.source_title: Activity Controls for Header Bidding (Prebid Server)

educate.learn_more_note: Microsoft Monetize's API authentication and credential management documentation is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Inventory the active API credentials on the account, confirm the scope each one holds against the job it performs, and check whether any rotation schedule exists.

monetize_ui.label: Microsoft Monetize console
steps: Open the API or credential management area of the Microsoft Monetize console and list every active credential or key issued for the account.
For each credential, record what system or integration uses it, who owns it, and the access scope it holds, whether read-only or write.
Flag any credential shared across multiple people or systems, and any credential whose scope exceeds the job it performs.
Record whether any credential has a rotation schedule and when each one was last rotated, and note any credential that has never been rotated.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each active API credential, the system that uses it, its owner, its access scope, and its last rotation date. A set of credentials where each one serves a single integration, holds only the scope its job requires, has a named owner, and follows a rotation schedule passes. Credentials that are shared across people or systems, scoped wider than the job, lacking a named owner, or never rotated are a fail recorded with the specific credentials and the gap each one shows.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each API credential, its scope, its owner, and its last rotation date above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that API credentials on {{site_name}}'s Microsoft Monetize account are shared across people or systems, scoped wider than the job they perform, or left without a rotation schedule. An API credential can change pricing, inventory, and demand settings at scale without a person watching, so a shared, over-scoped, or stale token carries administrative power with less accountability than a console seat. {{voice_recommend}} inventorying the active credentials, issuing a separate credential per integration scoped to the narrowest access its task requires, and assigning each one a named owner. {{voice_recommend}} setting a rotation schedule and replacing any credential whenever a person who knew it leaves, so an exposed key has a limited life rather than an open-ended one.
